bio.education: DeMatha High School (Hyattsville, Md.) ‘12 Syracuse University
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Full name is Houston Jerami Grant
  • Son of Harvey and Beverly Grant, has three brothers – Harvey Jerai, Holdyn Jerian and Harrison Jaelin
  • Jerai played college basketball for Clemson University and has played in professional leagues in Australia, Italy, Israel, Latvia and Lithuania

Career Highlights
  • Named a member of the 2016 USA Select Team that trained with and against the 2016 U.S. Olympic Team
  • Was one of three players in the NBA in 2018-2019 with more than 100 threes and 100 blocked shots
  • Scored his 3,000th career point at Utah on Feb. 22, 2019, becoming just the seventh forward since 2014 with 3,000-plus points and 400-plus blocked shots
  • Member of the 2012 USA U18 National Team that finished 5-0 and won gold at the FIBA Americas U18 Championship in Sao Sebastiao do Paraiso, Brazil; Started all five games, averaged 18.0 minutes, 5.6 points and 5.0 rebounds per game
  • Played two seasons at Syracuse University (2012-14)
Olympic Experience

    • Olympic Games Tokyo 2020, gold (Team - Men)

bio.education: West Monroe HS (1997) Oklahoma Christian University (2004; physical education and nutrition)
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Father's name is Benjamin Green, Sr. Mother's name is Lucille Carter
  • Has brothers named Keith, Dwayne, Ron, Stanley, Benjamin Jr. and Cordell
  • Has sisters named Devanna, Yavette, Treona, LaToya, Tameka and Wonda
  • The happiest moment of his life was helping deliver his daughter, Avayah, in 2013
  • Brother, Keith Earl, competed as a boxer from 1982-89
  • Founded a non-profit that helps people raise money for medical needs, mainly amputees, by holding basketball events to raise money
Career Highlights
  • 2019: Parapan American Games (Silver)
  • 2016: ParaVolley Intercontinental Cup (6th Place)
  • 2015: Parapan American Games (Silver)
  • 2012: Intercontinental Cup (8th place)
  • 2011: Parapan American Games (Silver); Continental Cup (8th place)
  • 2009: Parapan American Championship (Gold)
  • 2007: Parapan American Games (Silver)
  • ---
  • MOST NOTABLE
  • Named USA Volleyball 2016 Male Sitting Player of the Year
  • Has medaled at the Parapan American Games four times (1 Gold, 3x Silver)
  • Competed in the Sydney Paralympics and Athens Paralympics in track and field, winning one silver (200-meter dash) and two bronze medals (long jump; 400-meter dash)
  • ---
  • DID YOU KNOW?
  • Was born with genetic fibula hemimelia. His leg was amputated below the knee when he was 2 years old
  • Met his wife, Katrin, at the 2004 Paralympics where they were both track athletes. Katrin was competing for Germany. Katrin won the gold medal in the 200 meters at the 2008 Paralympics in Beijing and bronze in the 200-meter dash in 2012
  • Payed basketball on scholarship for three years in college
  • Roderick founded a non-profit that raises money to cover the cost of medical care for amputees

    • Paralympic Games Sydney 2000, silver (200m T44 - Men), bronze (400m T44 - Men, Long Jump T44 - Men), 5th (4 x 100m Relay T46 - Men)

  • Top finish: 6th – 2022 (Team - Men)

bio.education: Boys and Girls HS (Brooklyn, N.Y.) ‘95 DeVry University ’16, Sales and Marketing
bio.biography:
Height: 5-11
Weight: 200
Classification: LW2, Standing
Date of Birth: June 1, 1977
Birthplace: Brooklyn, N.Y.
Hometown: Brooklyn, N.Y.
Current Residence: Vail, Colo.
High School: Boys and Girls HS (Brooklyn, N.Y.) ‘95
College: DeVry University ’16, Sales and Marketing
Coach: Kevin Jardine

Career Highlights

  • 2014: Paralympic Winter Games, 17th giant slalom, 18th slalom; U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Championship, 1st slalom and giant slalom; Huntsman Cup, 1st slalom and giant slalom; World Cup Copper Mountain, 12th slalom run 1, 13th slalom run 2, 15th giant slalom run 1, 19th giant slalom run 2; World Cup Panorama, 20th giant slalom, 22nd super-G; NorAm Aspen, 1st downhill; NorAm Winter Park, 2nd giant slalom run 1, 3rd slalom and giant slalom run 2; U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Championship, 1st super-G, 2nd downhill
  • 2013: International Paralympic Committee Alpine Skiing World Championship, 8th downhill, 16th super-G; World Cup Mt. Hutt, 12th super-G
  • 2012: World Cup standings, 8th slalom, 12th super combined, 10th super-G; NorAm Copper Mountain, 2nd slalom, 4th giant slalom; NorAm Winter Park, 2nd giant slalom, 3rd super-G
  • 2011: IPC Alpine Skiing World Championship, 12th super combined, 14th downhill, 17th super-G; U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Championship, 1st super-G, super combined
  • 2010: 2010 Paralympic Winter Games, 22nd slalom, 29th giant slalom; IPC Alpine Skiing World Cup Finals, 5th downhill; U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Championship, 1st overall
  • 2009: IPC Alpine Skiing World Championship, 14th slalom, IPC Alpine Skiing World Cup Finals, 10th downhill; NorAm Park City, 4th slalom
  • 2008: U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Championship, 3rd downhill; NorAm Hartford, 2nd downhill; NorAm Copper Mountain, 4th slalom; The Hartford Ski Spectacular, Breckenridge, Colo., 4th slalom
  • 2006: Paralympic Winter Games, 36th slalom, 46th giant slalom
  • 2005: Four top-15 finishes in World Cup competition
  • 2004: IPC Alpine Skiing World Championship, 14th super-G; Two top-10 finishes in World Cup competition; Named to U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Team

Personal

Ralph Green didn’t give up after he lost a leg following a random street shooting. Coaches say he’s fearless and a hard worker. Green is the first African-American man to make the U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Team, and he represented Team USA at both the Torino 2006 and Vancouver 2010 Paralympic Winter Games.

A high school quarterback and multi-sport athlete, Green was gunned down at 16 while he and a friend were walking along a street in Brooklyn. His left leg was amputated at the hip. A year later, Green was introduced to skiing in the Poconos, and it didn’t do much for him. In 2000, though, Green found himself back on the slopes with a program in Winter Park, Colo., and as he says, “That’s when it stuck...‘I’m skiing until I’m the best.’” Green is now a 10-time U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Team member and four-time national champion. He will compete in his third straight Paralympic Winter Games with Team USA in Sochi. Off the slopes, Green enjoys cooking and competes in triathlons for cross-training.

Connect
Twitter: @bkskiman

Paralympic Experience
  • 3-time Paralympian

    • Paralympic Games Vancouver 2010, (Super-G - Standing - Men), 22th (Slalom - Standing - Men), 29th (Giant Slalom - Standing - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Turin 2006, (Downhill - Standing - Men, Super-G - Standing - Men), 36th (Slalom - Standing - Men), 46th (Giant Slalom - Standing - Men)
World Championships Experience

  • Years of participation: Downhill - Standing - Men 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015; Giant Slalom - Standing - Men 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015; Slalom - Standing - Men 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015; Super Combined - Standing - Men 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015; Super-G - Standing - Men 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015; Downhill LW2 - Men 2004; Giant Slalom LW2 - Men 2004; Slalom LW2 - Men 2004; Super-G LW2 - Men 2004
  • Top finish: – 2004 (Downhill LW2 - Men), 2004 (Giant Slalom LW2 - Men), 2004 (Slalom LW2 - Men), 2009 (Giant Slalom - Standing - Men), 2009 (Super Combined - Standing - Men), 2013 (Giant Slalom - Standing - Men), 2013 (Slalom - Standing - Men), 2013 (Super Combined - Standing - Men), 2015 (Giant Slalom - Standing - Men), 2015 (Slalom - Standing - Men), 2015 (Super Combined - Standing - Men)

Quick Facts
  • Son of Dagan and Shannon Greene
  • He has three siblings: Garrett, Jackson and Josie
  • Outside of diving, Bennett enjoys playing video games, trampolining and hanging out with friends
  • He trains two and a half hours day, five days a week
  • Bennett hopes to have a strong collegiate diving career with Olympic possibilities
  • He began diving at the age of five
  • He previously played baseball and basketball
  • He holds a 4.0 GPA
  • His first junior nationals was in 2013
  • His greatest diving obstacle was when he tore a ligament in his ankle prior to competing in the 2017 Junior Pan Am games. He captured 2 gold medals despite his setback
  • Between dives, Bennett likes to relax and talk to his coach
  • While competing, he likes to listen to hype music and rock and roll music
  • His favorite dives is 305c from the 7-meter platform
  • For the 2019 season, Bennett is focusing on gaining more flexibility and utilizing more strength. He hopes to medal in all three events at nationals and be invited to compete at Junior Pan Ams
  • Because his coach (Michael Wright) is notorious for difficult conditioning and core training, Bennett considers having sore muscles the most difficult part of his training
  • He typically has a protein bar as a pre-competition meal
  • His favorite meal outside of competition is Chick-fil-A
  • His favorite diver is David Boudia
  • He describes himself as positive, competitive and an encourager
  • His favorite t.v. show is The Simpsons. His favorite movie is Grown Ups
  • If Bennett could meet anyone it would be Adam Sandler
  • He occasionally does print and commercial modeling
Career Highlights
  • 2019 Junior Pan American Championships: silver medalist, 3-meter; bronze medal, platform
  • 2017 Junior Pan American Championships: gold medalist, 3-meter and platform
  • Four-time junior national champion (three titles on platform, one on 1-meter)

EXPERIENCE
2015 Junior Women's National Team Member
- World Cup of Softball participant
 - Hit .143 (2-for-14) with three RBI and two runs scored

AT TENNESSEE
NFCA All-America Scholar athlete (2015)
All-SEC Freshman Team (2015)
SEC All-Tournament Team (2015)
Top 25 Finalist for NFCA Freshman of the Year (2015)

GETTING TO KNOW GREGG
Parents: Kathy & Brad Gregg
Siblings: One brother, Stuart
Pets: Four cats
Favorite sport besides softball: Basketball
What it means to represent the United States: To be able to represent the United States in softball is such an honor. To have those letters across my chest has been my dream since I was a little girl and to make it a reality is just amazing. I have always looked up to players on the USA team and now I will be living that awesome opportunity.
What do you enjoy the most about softball:Playing softball is not only my hobby but also a huge part of my life. I have grown up in a very competitive family and loved sports. What I enjoy most about softball is the competiveness it brings. The results from that are the emotions that are created from playing the game. Nothing can compare to helping your team when they need it the most or knowing that I left all I had on the field for my teammates.
Who has been the biggest supporter of your career: This question cannot be answered with only person. My parents and grandparents are my biggest fans. I don’t remember a time that they have not been at a game in all my years growing up. They all have sacrificed so much time and effort for me to get where I am today. I am so thankful for my family.
Why she chose to attend Tennessee: I fell in love with how the women’s athletics were so important to the university. I also loved Ralph and Karen. Their coaching style was what I was used to.

TRIPLE PLAY:
Three people I'd love to have dinner with: Ellen DeGeneres, Melissa McCarthy, and Sandra Bullock
Three things I hate doing: timed running, riding in elevators with strangers, making my bed
Three things I love doing: having girls’ night, watching Sons of Anarchy, shopping
Three TV characters I wish were real so I could hang out with them: Jax Teller, Christian Gray, Damon Salvatore
Three favorite TV Shows: Orange is the New Black, Sons of Anarchy, The Following
Three favorite books: Great Gatsby, A Time to Kill, The Lucky One
Three favorite websites: Google, Netflix, Youtube
Career Highlights
  • World Cup, Irvine, 2015 (Team - Women)
